opencode-conversation-to-lino.py.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
opencode-conversation-to-lino.py

Extract the FULL conversation and metadata for an opencode session and render it
as Links Notation (indented object-tree style, matching link-assistant/formal-ai
data/seed/*.lino).

Why this exists
---------------
When an agentic tool (opencode, claude, codex, ...) is driven by the Formal AI
provider and the user says "Report issue", the report should carry the ENTIRE
conversation context -- messages, tool calls, tokens, timings, provider/model,
working directory -- not a bare natural-language summary. This script produces
that context in Links Notation so it is human-reviewable and machine-parseable,
the same reviewable text format Formal AI already uses for seed data and traces.

opencode (>= ~1.18) stores everything in a single SQLite database, NOT as
per-session JSON files:

    ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db

    session(id, project_id, slug, directory, title, version, agent, model,
            cost, tokens_*, time_created, time_updated, ...)
    message(id, session_id, time_created, time_updated, data)   -- data = JSON
    part(id, message_id, session_id, time_created, time_updated, data) -- JSON

Usage
-----
    ./opencode-conversation-to-lino.py <session_id> [--db PATH] [-o OUT.lino]
    ./opencode-conversation-to-lino.py ses_078007767ffecjImFV0GYvoKQu

    # override DB location or write to stdout (default)
    ./opencode-conversation-to-lino.py <id> --db ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db

Design notes
------------
* Read-only: opens the DB with mode=ro so a running opencode is never disturbed.
* No third-party deps -- stdlib sqlite3 + json only.
* Deterministic output: rows ordered by (time_created, id); dict keys sorted.
* Ordered collections use the native Links Notation SEQUENCE idiom -- the
  singular header key repeated once per element (`messages` -> repeated
  `message`, `parts` -> repeated `part`), no `_0.._N` index keys and no
  parentheses. Parentheses are reserved for inline scalar lists (`key (a b c)`).
  This shape is verified to round-trip through the `links-notation` grammar.

Sample output shape:

    conversation ses_XXXX
      messages
        message
          role user
          parts
            part
              data
                text "Find hive-mind-control center folder on my desktop"
                type text
        message
          role assistant
          parts
            part
              data
                type tool
                tool websearch
                state
                  status completed
                  input
                    query "hive mind control center folder on my desktop"
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import json
import os
import sqlite3
import sys
from typing import Any

INDENT = "  "  # two spaces, matching formal-ai .lino seed files


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
# Links Notation emitter
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
def _flatten(s: str) -> str:
    """Keep a value on one physical line (the grammar is line-oriented)."""
    return s.replace("\r", "\\r").replace("\n", "\\n").replace("\t", "\\t")


def _quote(s: str) -> str:
    """
    Render a value as a quoted Links Notation string using DELIMITER SELECTION,
    the same rule `lino-objects-codec` applies: pick a delimiter the value does
    not itself contain, so the delimiter never has to be escaped inside a value.

      * value has no `"`              -> wrap in double quotes  "like this"
      * value has `"` but no `'`      -> wrap in single quotes  'like "this"'
      * value has both `"` and `'`    -> double quotes with the embedded double
                                         quote DOUBLED (`""`), the grammar's own
                                         quote escape

    A value containing BOTH quote characters falls back to base64 (tagged
    `b64:`). The doubled-quote escape (`""`) is the grammar's own, but it was
    verified to desync the parser inside long, newline-escaped tool outputs when
    it accumulates across a whole document; base64 output is pure
    `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]`, so it carries no grammar-significant character and cannot
    desync. This mirrors how `lino-objects-codec` base64-encodes awkward values.

    The value is first flattened onto one physical line (CR/LF/TAB escaped),
    because the notation is line-oriented. Verified to round-trip through the
    `links-notation` grammar on the full conversation, including tool outputs
    that mix quotes, colons, and newline escapes.
    """
    flat = _flatten(s)
    if '"' not in flat:
        return f'"{flat}"'
    if "'" not in flat:
        return f"'{flat}'"
    import base64

    encoded = base64.b64encode(s.encode("utf-8")).decode("ascii")
    return f'"b64:{encoded}"'


# A value may be written as a BARE token (unquoted) only if every character is
# in this safe set. Everything else -- crucially `:` (structurally significant
# in Links Notation, so a bare `https://...` or `a:b` desyncs the parser),
# whitespace, quotes, parentheses -- forces quoting via `_quote`.
_BARE_SAFE = set(
    "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ0123456789_.-/"
)


def _needs_quote(s: str) -> bool:
    """A value is safe bare only if non-empty and all chars are bare-safe."""
    if s == "":
        return True
    return any(c not in _BARE_SAFE for c in s)


def _scalar(value: Any) -> str:
    if value is None:
        return "null"
    if isinstance(value, bool):
        return "true" if value else "false"
    if isinstance(value, (int, float)):
        return repr(value)
    s = str(value)
    return _quote(s) if _needs_quote(s) else s


# A list under one of these keys is an ORDERED SEQUENCE of records: it renders
# as the singular item key repeated once per element (native Links Notation
# idiom -- see experiments/issue_715_nested_newline_probe.rs, where a list of
# records is `expression / ... / expression / ...`). No _0.._N index keys, no
# parentheses. Any other key holding scalars renders inline as `key (a b c)`.
SEQUENCE_ITEM_NAME = {
    "messages": "message",
    "parts": "part",
}


def _item_name(key: str) -> str:
    """Singular header for one element of the sequence named `key`."""
    if key in SEQUENCE_ITEM_NAME:
        return SEQUENCE_ITEM_NAME[key]
    return key[:-1] if key.endswith("s") and len(key) > 1 else f"{key}_item"


def emit(node: Any, key: str | None, depth: int, out: list[str]) -> None:
    """
    Recursively render a Python value as Links Notation lines, preferring the
    multiline notation and using as few parentheses as possible.

    * dict  -> `key` header line, then one child line per item
    * list of records (dicts/lists) -> `key` header, then the singular item key
      repeated once per element as sibling records (a native LiNo sequence)
    * list of scalars -> inline `key (a b c)` (the one place parens are used)
    * scalar -> `key value`
    """
    pad = INDENT * depth

    if isinstance(node, dict):
        if key is not None:
            out.append(f"{pad}{key}")
        child_depth = depth + 1 if key is not None else depth
        for k in sorted(node.keys()):
            emit(node[k], str(k), child_depth, out)
        return

    if isinstance(node, list):
        head = key if key is not None else "list"
        # Scalars-only -> the sole parenthesized inline form.
        if all(not isinstance(x, (dict, list)) for x in node):
            inline = " ".join(_scalar(x) for x in node)
            out.append(f"{pad}{head} ({inline})")
            return
        # A sequence of records -> repeated singular-key siblings, no indices.
        out.append(f"{pad}{head}")
        item = _item_name(head)
        for element in node:
            emit(element, item, depth + 1, out)
        return

    # scalar
    if key is None:
        out.append(f"{pad}{_scalar(node)}")
    else:
        out.append(f"{pad}{key} {_scalar(node)}")


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
# DB access
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
def default_db_path() -> str:
    xdg = os.environ.get("XDG_DATA_HOME")
    base = xdg if xdg else os.path.expanduser("~/.local/share")
    return os.path.join(base, "opencode", "opencode.db")


def open_ro(db_path: str) -> sqlite3.Connection:
    if not os.path.exists(db_path):
        sys.exit(f"error: opencode database not found: {db_path}")
    uri = f"file:{db_path}?mode=ro"
    conn = sqlite3.connect(uri, uri=True)
    conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row
    return conn


def loads(blob: Any) -> Any:
    if blob is None:
        return None
    try:
        return json.loads(blob)
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        return {"_raw": str(blob)}


def fetch_session(conn: sqlite3.Connection, session_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
    row = conn.execute(
        "SELECT * FROM session WHERE id = ?", (session_id,)
    ).fetchone()
    if row is None:
        sys.exit(f"error: session not found: {session_id}")
    d = dict(row)
    # `model` column is itself a JSON string in some versions; expand it.
    if isinstance(d.get("model"), str) and d["model"].strip().startswith("{"):
        d["model"] = loads(d["model"])
    if isinstance(d.get("metadata"), str) and d["metadata"]:
        d["metadata"] = loads(d["metadata"])
    return d


def fetch_messages(conn: sqlite3.Connection, session_id: str) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    rows = conn.execute(
        "SELECT id, time_created, time_updated, data FROM message "
        "WHERE session_id = ? ORDER BY time_created, id",
        (session_id,),
    ).fetchall()
    return [
        {
            "id": r["id"],
            "time_created": r["time_created"],
            "time_updated": r["time_updated"],
            "data": loads(r["data"]),
        }
        for r in rows
    ]


def fetch_parts(conn: sqlite3.Connection, message_id: str) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    rows = conn.execute(
        "SELECT id, time_created, data FROM part "
        "WHERE message_id = ? ORDER BY time_created, id",
        (message_id,),
    ).fetchall()
    return [
        {"id": r["id"], "time_created": r["time_created"], "data": loads(r["data"])}
        for r in rows
    ]


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
# Build the object tree, then emit
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
def build_tree(conn: sqlite3.Connection, session_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
    session = fetch_session(conn, session_id)
    messages = fetch_messages(conn, session_id)

    # `messages` and `parts` are ORDERED SEQUENCES: plain lists, so the emitter
    # renders them as repeated `message` / `part` sibling records (no _0.._N
    # index keys). List order is the conversation/turn order; the record fields
    # inside each element may be key-sorted freely without changing meaning.
    msg_nodes: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
    for m in messages:
        data = m["data"] or {}
        parts = fetch_parts(conn, m["id"])
        part_nodes: list[dict[str, Any]] = [
            {
                "id": p["id"],
                "time_created": p["time_created"],
                "data": p["data"],
            }
            for p in parts
        ]
        msg_nodes.append(
            {
                "id": m["id"],
                "role": data.get("role"),
                "time_created": m["time_created"],
                "time_updated": m["time_updated"],
                "data": data,
                "parts": part_nodes,
                "part_count": len(parts),
            }
        )

    return {
        "source": {
            "tool": "opencode",
            "storage": "sqlite",
            "notation": "links-notation",
            "extractor": "opencode-conversation-to-lino.py",
        },
        "session": session,
        "message_count": len(messages),
        "messages": msg_nodes,
    }


def render(tree: dict[str, Any], session_id: str) -> str:
    out: list[str] = []
    # Root identifier line, then the whole tree as its children.
    out.append(f"conversation {session_id}")
    for key in ("source", "session", "message_count", "messages"):
        emit(tree[key], key, 1, out)
    return "\n".join(out) + "\n"


# --------------------------------------------------------------------------- #
def main() -> None:
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__.split("\n\n")[0])
    ap.add_argument("session_id", help="opencode session id, e.g. ses_XXXX")
    ap.add_argument("--db", default=default_db_path(), help="path to opencode.db")
    ap.add_argument(
        "-o", "--output", default="-", help="output .lino file (default: stdout)"
    )
    args = ap.parse_args()

    conn = open_ro(os.path.expanduser(args.db))
    try:
        tree = build_tree(conn, args.session_id)
    finally:
        conn.close()

    text = render(tree, args.session_id)
    if args.output == "-":
        sys.stdout.write(text)
    else:
        with open(os.path.expanduser(args.output), "w", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
            fh.write(text)
        print(f"wrote {args.output}", file=sys.stderr)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

description: Formal AI agentic reporting: extract full opencode conversation + metadata from opencode.db and render as Links Notation (link-assistant/formal-ai). Read-only, stdlib-only.
